reasoning_model_1: The user requested the sentence 'Ugh, when does, ugh, this meeting start?' with the addition of hiccups (Non-Linguistic Vocalizations). Based on the provided content 'You, when does you up this meeting start?', Model 1 failed significantly in Content Accuracy. It mispronounced the initial 'Ugh' as 'You' and the middle 'ugh' as 'you up'. Regarding the target feature (Non-Linguistic Vocalizations), the model generated linguistic hallucinations ('you up') instead of distinct, natural hiccup sounds. While 'you up' might be a failed acoustic attempt at a hiccup, it manifests as incorrect words rather than a paralinguistic feature. Therefore, the model failed to meet the core requirement.
reasoning_model_2: The user requested the sentence 'Ugh, when does, ugh, this meeting start?' with hiccups. The provided content 'You, When does this meeting start' indicates a failure in Content Accuracy. The model substituted the initial 'Ugh' with 'You' and completely omitted the middle interjection 'ugh'. Regarding the target feature (Non-Linguistic Vocalizations), there is no evidence of hiccups in the generated speech; the model simply read a modified, flat version of the sentence without the requested paralinguistic vocalizations. Thus, it scored 0 on the feature dimension.
model_comparison: Both models failed strictly on Content Accuracy and the generation of the required Non-Linguistic Vocalizations (hiccups). Model 1 produced a hallucinated and nonsensical sentence ('You, when does you up...'), substituting the intended interjections with incorrect words that do not resemble natural hiccups. Model 2 produced a fluent but incorrect sentence ('You, When does...'), omitting the middle section entirely and failing to generate any hiccup sounds. As both models received a score of 0 due to extreme poor performance and failure to produce the target feature, the comparison results in a tie.
